text: The hiatal hernia is visible at the top of the screen. The surgeon uses a cautery instrument to dissect and remove fatty tissue from the hernia sac, preparing the area for repair.

The robotic system precisely dissects the tissue surrounding the diaphragm, esophagus, and stomach, achieving minimal bleeding and providing a clear, high-definition, three-dimensional view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses cautery to seal tiny blood vessels without bleeding while carefully dissecting around both sides of the esophagus, ensuring the nerve remains unharmed.

The nerve is visible at the top of the screen. The robotic system is used to mobilize the esophagus, allowing the placement of an instrument behind it, which is necessary for the dissection and where the wrap will be positioned later.
The surgical view reveals the left and right diaphragm crura bordering the hiatal hernia. At the top of the dissection, the pulsating white vessel visible at twelve o'clock is the aorta.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the short gastric blood vessels on the outside of the stomach, achieving a bloodless field with the heat sealing instrument.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the upper portion of the stomach, both anteriorly and posteriorly, creating a space behind the stomach necessary for the subsequent fundoplication procedure.
The surgeon continues the dissection, carefully dividing blood vessels attached to the spleen and surrounding fatty tissue using a vessel sealer, achieving minimal blood loss.
The robotic instruments demonstrate wristing movements as they maneuver around the tissue, showcasing their flexibility and precision in the surgical field.

Both sides of the stomach are visualized passing behind the esophagus, forming a wrap. The surgeon begins closing the diaphragm crura with sutures, carefully re-approximating the muscle to avoid crushing the tissue. A few sutures effectively close the defect, eliminating the hernia.
The surgeon discusses the closure of larger defects using sutures and mesh prosthetic material, although this reinforcement is not required for the current hernia repair.
